story:
El, the ex-wife with pitchfork-and-spade tattoos, stands at the lectern and refuses to tidy the truth. Jeanie could be a twat, she says, but also a night-owl grafter, a real artist who made tiny things with big dreams. El remembers the woman she first took for coffee, who loved The Glory, Hackney’s corners, and the serious behind the funny. Then her voice breaks: Where’d you go, Jeanie?

Before the vanishing, Jeanie stares into her full-length mirror, haunted by late nights, spider veins, and the churn of four jobs. She wishes for one life that fits. Her hand slips through the glass. On the other side: an emerald lawn, a glass house, and another Jeanie—same height, ember hair—filming “Saturday Tackquisition” for an army of followers. Jeanie shouts; no one hears. She can’t scuff the car paint, can’t leave a mark. Invisible, she wanders inside to a dream studio and a perfect miniature of the Hackney Empire, where El once said I bloody love you. Panic sends her sprinting back through the haze. Thirty minutes there, thirty here. A door between lives.

Soon she learns the mirror answers to wanting. Think of a life and it appears, real time and present tense. She buys an analogue watch to limit her trips. At first it’s “just half an hour,” then “just until lunchtime,” then “just when El will notice.” She misses orders, dodges questions. The space between them grows. Jeanie visits versions of herself she envies: a bar owner in Vietnam pouring fire for the ecstatic night; a cyclist conquering the Alps; a Berlin artist, riotous with paint; and, most of all, CJ—the war correspondent pulling truth from rubble, her eyes hard and human. Jeanie follows CJ to a Peabody stage, lying to El about a work trip to Manchester, just to stand in the warm light of achievement—someone else’s, but hers too.

Then, in Gabon, the air crackles. A gunman’s drive-by tears through press and refugees. Jeanie watches a bullet take CJ in the neck. She sees the life leave her own eyes. Dragged back by a wrongness she can’t fight, she stumbles into her bedroom and wills the mirror to reopen to CJ. It won’t. Her hands are small, unscarred, and useless.

El pleads for the truth; Jeanie won’t give it. When El ends it, Jeanie fears losing the mirror more than losing home. She steps through without a plan to return, not to live there, but to haunt its edges for hours at a time, always ducking back to her own flat to eat, sleep, and vanish again—until the weeks pile up and it feels like she’s been hiding for months in Rich-Jeanie’s attic of miniatures under a sky as big as her avoidance. She listens to RJ cook El’s favorite dim sum—Steak a Bao, Shrimply the Best Siu Mai—and thinks, I can be that person too.

Back home, time moves on. With no word and no body, there’s a memorial service—what everyone calls a funeral because grief needs closure. El tells the room Jeanie went long before she went. Later that day, El finds the kitchen light on. Jeanie stands there, and upstairs the mirror lies smashed, its spell done.

I’ve just done your fucking eulogy, El says.

I hope it was good.

I called you a twat.

Sounds fair.

And you don’t even have the good grace to be dead.

But I have been, Jeanie says, and breaks. El gathers her in, holding the only Jeanie she’s got, as the tiny, perfect worlds fall quiet and the real one waits.
